subject: Biology
question_text: Which organelle is responsible for producing ATP through aerobic respiration?
markscheme: The mitochondria is responsible for ATP production through aerobic respiration (oxidative phosphorylation). [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: The cell membrane is described as a 'fluid mosaic model'. What does 'fluid' refer to in this context?
option_a: The membrane can dissolve in water
option_b: Phospholipids can move laterally within the bilayer
option_c: The membrane allows water to pass freely
option_d: Proteins float on the outer surface only
markscheme: 'Fluid' refers to the ability of phospholipids to move laterally within the bilayer, giving the membrane flexibility. [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: Describe the stages of mitosis and explain the significance of each stage for the production of genetically identical daughter cells.
marks:
Award marks for the following points:
• Prophase: chromosomes condense and become visible; nuclear envelope breaks down; spindle fibres form [1]
• Metaphase: chromosomes align at the metaphase plate (equator); spindle fibres attach to centromeres [1]
• Anaphase: sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles by shortening spindle fibres [1]
• Telophase: nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes; chromosomes decondense [1]
• Cytokinesis: cytoplasm divides to produce two genetically identical daughter cells [1]
• Each stage ensures equal distribution of genetic material, resulting in daughter cells with the same chromosome number and DNA sequence as the parent cell [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: State three differences between DNA and RNA.
marks:
Any three of the following [1 each]:
• DNA is double-stranded; RNA is single-stranded
• DNA contains deoxyribose sugar; RNA contains ribose sugar
• DNA contains thymine (T); RNA contains uracil (U)
• DNA is found mainly in the nucleus; RNA is found in cytoplasm and nucleus
• DNA is longer/larger; RNA is shorter/smaller

subject: Biology
question_text: Outline the role of the following enzymes in DNA replication: (i) helicase, (ii) DNA polymerase.
marks:
(i) Helicase: unwinds the double helix and breaks hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs, separating the two strands [1]
(ii) DNA polymerase: adds complementary nucleotides in the 5' to 3' direction along each template strand; joins nucleotides by phosphodiester bonds [1]
Accept: proofreads newly synthesised DNA [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: In pea plants, round seed shape (R) is dominant over wrinkled (r). A cross is made between two heterozygous plants (Rr × Rr). What proportion of offspring will have wrinkled seeds?
markscheme: From a Rr × Rr cross, the genotype ratio is 1 RR : 2 Rr : 1 rr. Only rr (1/4) will show the wrinkled phenotype as wrinkled is recessive. [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: Explain the process of translation, including the roles of mRNA, tRNA, and ribosomes.
marks:
Award marks for the following points:
• mRNA (messenger RNA) carries the genetic code from the nucleus to the ribosome as a sequence of codons (triplets of bases) [1]
• Ribosome binds to the start codon (AUG) on mRNA [1]
• tRNA molecules carry specific amino acids; each tRNA has an anticodon complementary to a mRNA codon [1]
• tRNA anticodon base-pairs with complementary mRNA codon at the ribosome A-site [1]
• Peptide bonds form between adjacent amino acids; ribosome moves along mRNA in 5'→3' direction [1]
• Process continues until a stop codon is reached; polypeptide chain is released [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: In a food web, which organisms are always found at the base (first trophic level)?
markscheme: Producers (autotrophs) such as plants and algae are always at the base of food webs. They convert light energy into chemical energy via photosynthesis. [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: Explain why energy transfer between trophic levels is inefficient. State the approximate percentage of energy transferred.
marks:
Only approximately 10% of energy is transferred between trophic levels [1]
Energy is lost as:
• Heat from cellular respiration [1]
• Material in faeces/urine that is not absorbed [1]
• Accept: energy used in movement/other metabolic processes [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: Compare and contrast abiotic and biotic factors that affect population size in an ecosystem. Give two examples of each.
marks:
Abiotic factors (non-living/physical environmental factors):
• Examples: temperature, rainfall, light intensity, soil pH, salinity, wind [1 for any two]
• Affect population by influencing survival, reproduction, and resource availability [1]

Biotic factors (living/biological factors):
• Examples: predation, competition, disease, parasitism, food availability [1 for any two]
• Affect population through interspecific/intraspecific interactions [1]

Comparison:
• Both can limit population growth (limiting factors) [1]
• Abiotic factors are density-independent; many biotic factors are density-dependent [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: State the four key observations that Darwin used to develop his theory of natural selection.
marks:
All four required for full marks [1 each]:
1. Organisms produce more offspring than can survive (overproduction)
2. There is variation in individuals within a population
3. Some variations are heritable (passed to offspring)
4. Individuals with favourable variations are more likely to survive and reproduce (differential survival/reproduction)

subject: Biology
question_text: Describe three types of evidence that support the theory of evolution.
marks:
Any three [2 marks each, 6 total]:

Fossil record: Fossils show gradual change in organisms over time; transitional fossils link ancestral and descendant forms; older rocks contain simpler organisms [2]

Comparative anatomy: Homologous structures (e.g., pentadactyl limb in vertebrates) show common ancestry despite different functions; vestigial structures show evolutionary relationships [2]

Molecular biology: Similarities in DNA/protein sequences between species indicate common ancestry; more closely related species share more DNA similarities [2]

Biogeography: Distribution of species across continents reflects evolutionary history and continental drift [2]

subject: Biology
question_text: An enzyme is denatured at high temperatures. Which statement best explains this?
option_a: The active site shape changes permanently due to disruption of hydrogen bonds and other bonds
option_b: The enzyme runs out of substrate
option_c: The reaction rate decreases because molecules move more slowly
option_d: The pH of the solution increases
markscheme: At high temperatures, hydrogen bonds and other interactions holding the tertiary structure are disrupted, causing the active site to change shape permanently (denaturation). The enzyme can no longer bind substrate. [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: Write the overall equation for photosynthesis using chemical formulae.
marks:
6CO2 + 6H2O → C6H12O6 + 6O2 [1]
(light energy must be indicated above the arrow or stated) [1]

subject: Biology
question_text: Explain the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis, including the products formed.
marks:
Award marks for the following [1 each, max 6]:
• Light energy is absorbed by chlorophyll/photosystems in the thylakoid membranes [1]
• Water is split (photolysis): H2O → 2H+ + ½O2 + 2e- [1]
• Oxygen is released as a by-product [1]
• Excited electrons pass along the electron transport chain [1]
• ATP is synthesised by photophosphorylation (chemiosmosis via ATP synthase) [1]
• NADPH is produced when electrons reduce NADP+ [1]
• ATP and NADPH are the products that drive the light-independent (Calvin cycle) reactions [1]
